questions.explanation: The symptom pattern — works for you, fails in CI AND for a fresh clone — is the classic configuration-hierarchy diagnosis: the criteria live in user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, which is not version-controlled and never reaches the CI runner or teammates. Moving them to project-level CLAUDE.md (committed to the repo) fixes the root cause, and /memory is the documented diagnostic for verifying which memory files actually load. Option A patches the symptom in CI infrastructure but leaves teammates broken and creates config drift between the image and the repo. Option B references a CLAUDE_RULES environment variable that does not exist. Option D duplicates project context into every prompt, bypassing the mechanism CLAUDE.md exists for and requiring every script and developer to maintain the copy.

questions.rule: On re-review after new commits, feed the prior run's findings into context and instruct Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues.
questions.stem: Your CI review bot runs on every push to a PR. After a developer pushes commits fixing 9 of the 11 issues from the first review, the second run reposts all 11 original comments plus 2 new ones, and developers are starting to complain about comment spam. What change would most effectively address this?
options: Include the prior run's findings in the new run's context and instruct Claude to report only issues that are new or previously reported and still unfixed.
Add a post-processing step to the comment-posting script that string-matches new findings against existing PR comments and suppresses near-duplicates.
Resume the first review's session with --resume so the model retains its earlier findings and naturally avoids repeating them.
Delete all existing bot comments at the start of each run and repost the current findings as a clean slate.
questions.explanation: The model reposts old findings because each CI run is stateless — it has no knowledge of what previous runs reported. Including the prior findings in context with an explicit 'only new or still-unaddressed' instruction fixes that root cause and lets the model reason about whether each old issue was actually resolved by the new commits. Option B patches the symptom downstream: string matching can't tell a still-unfixed issue from a resolved one, and rewording defeats the match. Option C resumes a session whose tool results are stale — the code has changed since that analysis, which is exactly when starting fresh with injected context is more reliable than resuming. Option D still notifies developers with a wall of reposted comments and destroys the comment-thread history where discussion happened.

questions.rule: Test-generation quality comes from context: provide existing test files plus valuable-test criteria and fixture docs in CLAUDE.md — the model can't avoid duplicating what it can't see.
questions.stem: Your nightly test-generation job produces suggestions developers reject at a 60% rate: roughly half duplicate scenarios already covered in tests/unit/, and the rest are trivial getter/setter tests. The job currently receives only the source file under test in its prompt. What change would most effectively improve acceptance?
options: Add a post-generation similarity filter that computes embedding distance between each proposed test and the existing suite, discarding matches above a threshold.
Add the instruction 'do not propose tests that already exist, and skip trivial tests' to the generation prompt.
Switch the nightly job to a higher-tier model with a larger context window so it can reason more carefully about test value.
Include the module's existing test files in the prompt, and document valuable-test criteria and available fixtures in the project CLAUDE.md.
questions.explanation: Both failure modes are missing-context problems. The model duplicates existing scenarios because it has never seen the existing suite, and it produces low-value tests because nothing defines what a valuable test is for this team. Providing existing test files in context plus documented testing standards and fixtures in CLAUDE.md is the documented mechanism for exactly these two failures. Option B is prompt-only compliance against information the model doesn't have — it cannot avoid duplicating tests it can't see. Option A is over-engineered downstream filtering that discards wasted generations instead of preventing them, and does nothing about trivial tests. Option C misdiagnoses the problem as model capability: no model can check against a test suite that isn't in its context.

questions.rule: Machine-parseable CI findings require --output-format json with --json-schema — prompt-level 'return JSON' requests are probabilistic and unenforced.
questions.stem: Your CI review step pipes Claude Code's output into a script that posts inline PR comments. The prompt ends with 'Respond only with a JSON array of findings,' but in roughly 8% of runs the script crashes: the output contains markdown fences around the JSON, an introductory sentence, or a missing field. What's the correct fix?
options: Set CLAUDE_OUTPUT_FORMAT=json in the pipeline environment so Claude Code serializes its response before printing.
Invoke Claude Code with --output-format json and --json-schema pointing to a committed findings schema, then parse the structured result.
Harden the posting script to strip markdown fences and leading prose with a regex before parsing, and retry the run on parse failure.
Strengthen the prompt with an explicit warning that any non-JSON output will break the pipeline and cause the review to fail.
questions.explanation: The root cause is that JSON was requested through prompt text, which is probabilistic — nothing enforces the format. The documented CI mechanism is --output-format json combined with --json-schema, which produces machine-parseable, schema-enforced findings and eliminates the syntax failures crashing the script. Option A references an environment variable that does not exist. Option C patches symptoms downstream: regex stripping handles the failure shapes you've seen but not the next one (e.g., a missing required field), and retries burn CI time on a problem the CLI already solves deterministically. Option D is still prompt-only compliance — a sterner warning changes the probability, not the guarantee, and an 8% failure rate on a blocking pipeline needs a guarantee.

questions.rule: A session that generated code retains its generation reasoning and won't question its own decisions — review must run in a fresh, independent instance.
questions.stem: Your pipeline uses Claude Code to apply automated dependency-upgrade refactors, then asks the same session to review its own diff before opening the PR. Human spot-checks find the self-review approves changes containing real regressions in about 1 in 5 PRs, even though the review prompt says 'scrutinize this diff as if a different engineer wrote it.' What change would most effectively catch these regressions?
options: Run the review as a separate CI step in a fresh Claude Code session that receives only the diff and repo context, with no access to the generator session.
Enable extended thinking for the self-review step so the model reasons more deeply before approving its own changes.
Strengthen the review prompt to instruct the model to adopt an adversarial reviewer persona and list at least three potential problems before approving.
Run the self-review three times in the same session and only approve the PR when all three runs agree the diff is safe.
questions.explanation: The root cause is session context: an instance that generated the code retains the reasoning that produced its decisions, making it systematically unlikely to question them. Independent review instances without the generator's reasoning context catch issues that self-review instructions and extended thinking do not — and in CI, a fresh session per step is free. Option B applies more compute to a biased context; deeper reasoning from the same premises reproduces the same blind spots. Option C is prompt-only role-play that the stem shows is already being tried and failing — the persona instruction doesn't remove the generation context. Option D repeats the same biased evaluation three times; correlated errors agree with each other, so consensus among self-reviews launders the bias rather than catching it.

questions.rule: Precision comes from explicit categorical report/skip criteria, not from vague caution instructions or confidence filtering.
questions.stem: Your PR review bot's prompt says 'check that error handling is robust and flag anything concerning.' Developers report that 45% of its error-handling findings are false positives — mostly flagging intentional fire-and-forget patterns that match the surrounding codebase. What prompt change would most effectively reduce the false positive rate?
options: Add 'be conservative and only flag issues you are highly confident about' to the review instructions.
Have the model attach a confidence score to each finding and configure the posting script to suppress findings below 0.8.

Replace the vague instruction with explicit criteria defining which error-handling issues to report (e.g., swallowed exceptions on payment or data-write paths) and which to skip (e.g., patterns consistent with the surrounding codebase).
Add a second Claude pass that re-evaluates each finding from the first pass and filters out those it judges to be false positives.
questions.explanation: The false positives stem from a vague criterion ('robust', 'anything concerning') that gives the model no decision boundary, so it flags patterns the team considers acceptable. Explicit categorical report/skip criteria — including skipping patterns consistent with the local codebase — directly define that boundary. Option A is the named trap: general instructions like 'be conservative' fail to improve precision because they don't tell the model what conservative means for this codebase. Option B relies on self-reported confidence, an unreliable proxy — the model is confidently wrong about exactly these patterns. Option D adds cost and latency to filter outputs downstream while the generator keeps producing noise from the same vague criterion, and the filter inherits the same undefined standard for what counts as a real issue.

questions.rule: When detailed instructions still produce inconsistently formatted output, 2-4 few-shot examples demonstrating the exact desired format are the most effective fix.
questions.stem: Your review prompt contains a detailed specification of the required finding format: file location, issue description, severity, and a suggested fix. Despite this, about 30% of findings arrive malformed — severity written as a sentence, suggested fixes merged into the issue description, or the location omitted. What change would most effectively achieve consistent output?
options: Expand the format specification with a numbered list of formatting rules and a warning that malformed findings will be discarded.
Add 2-4 few-shot examples of complete, correctly formatted findings, including one demonstrating how to format an ambiguous case.
Lower the sampling temperature for the review invocation so the model adheres more deterministically to the written specification.
Split the review into two calls: one that identifies issues in free text, and a second that reformats that text into the required structure.
questions.explanation: Few-shot examples are the most effective technique for achieving consistently formatted, actionable output precisely when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results — the situation described. Concrete examples demonstrate the format unambiguously and generalize to novel findings, including ambiguous cases. Option A adds more of the thing that is already failing: prose specifications are interpreted inconsistently no matter how numbered or threatening. Option C misdiagnoses formatting drift as a sampling problem; temperature doesn't teach the model what the format is. Option D over-engineers a two-call pipeline with doubled cost and latency to work around a problem a few examples solve in one call, and the reformatting call would itself need format examples to be reliable.

questions.rule: When one category generates most false positives, temporarily disable it while improving its prompt — high-FP categories destroy trust in the accurate categories too.
questions.stem: Analysis of dismissed findings shows your review bot's 'documentation accuracy' category accounts for 70% of all false positives, while its security and bug categories run above 90% precision. Developers have begun bulk-dismissing every bot comment, including valid critical security findings. What's the most effective immediate response?
options: Add 'only report documentation issues when you are highly confident' to the documentation category's criteria.
Apply a global confidence threshold across all categories so only the strongest findings in any category get posted.
Reclassify all documentation-accuracy findings as minor severity so developers can filter them out of their review workflow.
Temporarily disable the documentation-accuracy category while you rewrite its criteria, keeping security and bug reporting active.
questions.explanation: The urgent problem is trust contagion: one high-false-positive category is causing developers to dismiss the accurate categories' findings too, including critical security issues. The documented remedy is to temporarily disable the offending category while its prompt is improved — this immediately stops the noise, protects trust in the >90%-precision categories, and loses only a category that was mostly wrong anyway. Option A is the 'high-confidence' caution instruction that fails to improve precision. Option B punishes the healthy categories: a global threshold suppresses valid security findings to treat a problem caused by one category. Option C solves a different problem — the findings are still posted and still mostly wrong; labeling noise 'minor' doesn't restore trust, it teaches developers the bot mislabels on top of misfiring.

questions.rule: Strict schemas eliminate JSON syntax errors, not semantic errors — semantic failures need validation plus retry-with-error-feedback.
questions.stem: Since adding --json-schema, your review findings parse successfully in 100% of runs. But developers report that about 15% of inline comments land on the wrong line, and some findings carry severities that contradict your documented anchors. Your team is confused because 'the schema should have prevented this.' What should you do?
options: Add programmatic validation after each run (e.g., verify each file/line exists in the diff, check severity against the anchor rules) and on failure retry with the specific validation errors appended to the prompt.
Tighten the JSON schema by adding stricter type constraints, minimum/maximum bounds on the line field, and pattern validation on file paths.
Force the findings tool with tool_choice so the model must emit findings through the schema-bound tool on every invocation.
Drop the schema and have the model write findings as free-form prose, since the structured format is constraining its reasoning about locations.
questions.explanation: This is the schema boundary: strict schemas guarantee the output parses (syntax) but cannot guarantee the values are right (semantics) — a line number can be a perfectly valid integer and still point at the wrong line. Semantic errors require external validation plus the retry-with-error-feedback pattern: re-prompt with the specific validation failures so the model can self-correct. Option B stays inside the schema, which can only check form: a line-number bound can't know which line the issue is actually on. Option C solves a different problem — tool_choice guarantees the tool gets called, but the tool is already being called; the values inside it are what's wrong. Option D is a regression to the pre-schema world, reintroducing the parse failures the schema eliminated while doing nothing for location accuracy.

questions.rule: Batch failures are handled per custom_id: resubmit only the failed requests, modified to fix the failure cause — never rerun the whole batch.
questions.stem: Your nightly test-generation batch submits 400 source modules through the Message Batches API. This morning's results show 352 succeeded, while 48 failed because several large modules exceeded the context limit. What's the correct way to handle the failures?
options: Resubmit the full 400-request batch with the oversized modules removed, then process those modules manually.
Move the 48 failed modules to synchronous API calls against a higher-tier model with a larger context window.
Identify the 48 failed requests by their custom_id values, chunk the oversized modules, and resubmit only those requests in a new batch.
Configure the nightly job to automatically resubmit the entire batch each night until every request in it succeeds.
questions.explanation: custom_id exists precisely for correlating batch requests with results, so failures can be isolated and resubmitted individually — with modifications that address why they failed, here chunking the modules that blew the context limit. Option A re-pays for 352 requests that already succeeded and abandons the failed modules to manual work instead of fixing them. Option B is over-engineered escalation: it forfeits the 50% batch savings and adds a second code path for a latency-tolerant overnight job, when chunking within the existing batch lane solves the actual failure cause. Option D blindly re-pays for the entire batch every night, and the oversized modules will fail identically forever because nothing about the requests changed.

questions.rule: Fields the source document may not contain must be optional/nullable — a required field the document lacks pressures the model to fabricate a value.
questions.stem: Your invoice extraction pipeline uses tool use with a strict JSON schema in which every field is required. QA sampling finds that for the 9% of invoices that print no purchase-order number, extractions contain plausible-looking PO numbers that appear nowhere in the source document. Schema validation passes on every one of these. What change would most effectively eliminate the fabricated values?
options: Add a system-prompt instruction: "Never guess or infer values; only extract information explicitly present in the document"
Add a verification pass in which a second independent Claude instance checks each extracted PO number against the document text before acceptance
Make po_number nullable in the schema and state in the tool description that the model must return null when the field is absent from the document
Enable strict mode on the JSON schema so the model can only emit values grounded in the input document
questions.explanation: A required schema field the document lacks forces the model to invent a value to satisfy the schema — the fabrication is structural, not behavioral. Making the field nullable with a return-null instruction in the tool description removes that pressure at the root, which is the guide's stated fix for exactly this failure. The system-prompt instruction is probabilistic compliance layered on a schema that still demands a value, so the structural pressure remains. The second-instance verification pass detects fabrications downstream at double the cost and latency instead of preventing them — and the extraction still needs a way to represent absence, which is the nullable field. Strict mode is a misattributed capability: strict schemas eliminate JSON syntax errors, but no schema mode grounds values in the source text.

questions.rule: Guaranteed schema-compliant structured output = tool use with the JSON schema as input_schema, never JSON-by-prompting plus cleanup.
questions.stem: Your extraction service prompts Claude to "respond with a JSON object matching this schema" and parses the text response. In production, 6% of responses fail json.loads — markdown code fences, explanatory preambles before the object, and trailing commas — crashing the downstream ingestion job. What change would most effectively eliminate these failures?
options: Add a post-processing repair layer that strips code fences, removes preamble text, and fixes trailing commas before parsing
Define an extraction tool whose input_schema is the output schema, force it with tool_choice, and read the structured data from the tool_use block's input
Strengthen the prompt to "Respond ONLY with valid JSON — no markdown, no commentary" and retry any response that fails to parse
Set output_format: "json" on the Messages API request to enforce JSON-only responses
questions.explanation: Tool use with the JSON schema as the tool's input_schema eliminates syntax failures by construction — the model's tool arguments must conform to the schema, so fences, preambles, and trailing commas cannot occur. This is the guide's stated most-reliable approach and the root-cause fix. The repair layer patches symptoms, chasing an open-ended set of malformations it will keep missing. The strengthened prompt plus retry is probabilistic compliance with paid retries for a failure class tool use removes entirely. The output_format parameter is a nonexistent Messages API feature — structured output on the API is achieved through tool use, not an output-format flag.

questions.rule: Multiple extraction schemas with unknown document type → tool_choice "any": guarantees a tool call while letting the model pick the matching schema.
questions.stem: Your pipeline registers three extraction tools — record_invoice, record_contract, record_receipt — because document type is unknown at submission time. tool_choice is currently {"type": "auto"}. In 4% of runs the model returns prose ("This appears to be a receipt from...") instead of calling any tool, and the parser crashes. What change most effectively guarantees structured output?
options: Set tool_choice to {"type": "tool", "name": "record_invoice"} since invoices are the most common document type
Add a system-prompt instruction: "You must always call exactly one extraction tool; never respond with plain text"
Add a classification pre-pass that calls the model once to detect the document type, then makes a second call forcing the corresponding tool
Set tool_choice to {"type": "any"} so the model must call a tool but can select the schema matching the document type
questions.explanation: tool_choice "any" deterministically guarantees a tool call while preserving the model's ability to choose the schema that matches the document — the guide's prescribed setting for exactly this case: multiple extraction schemas with unknown document type. Forcing record_invoice pushes every contract and receipt through the wrong schema, corrupting the data to fix the format. The system-prompt mandate is probabilistic — under "auto" the model may still return text, so the 4% failure mode persists at some rate. The classification pre-pass doubles calls and latency to rebuild, outside the API, routing that "any" already provides deterministically in a single call.

questions.rule: Strict JSON schemas eliminate syntax errors only; semantic errors require code-level cross-field validation plus retry-with-error-feedback.
questions.stem: Every extraction in your pipeline passes strict JSON schema validation, yet finance reports that in 3% of invoices the line-item amounts do not sum to the extracted total, and in some documents the ship-to address lands in the bill-to field. What change would most effectively address these errors?
options: Add code-level semantic validation (cross-field checks such as line-item sums), and on failure send a follow-up request containing the document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error
Tighten the JSON schema with stricter types, patterns, and additionalProperties: false so invalid combinations cannot validate
Upgrade the extraction calls to a higher-tier model with stronger reasoning to reduce extraction mistakes
Run each extraction three times and accept the majority value for each field, discarding outlier extractions
questions.explanation: Line items that fail to sum and swapped same-typed fields are semantic errors — schema-valid values that are wrong in relation to each other, which the guide is explicit that strict schemas cannot prevent. The fix is a code-level semantic validation layer (e.g., Pydantic cross-field checks) plus retry-with-error-feedback carrying the document, the failed extraction, and the specific error so the model can self-correct. Schema tightening misunderstands the boundary: no JSON schema construct expresses cross-field arithmetic or detects a value placed in the wrong same-typed field. A higher-tier model is disproportionate spend that at best reduces, never guarantees against, errors a deterministic check catches for free. Triple extraction with majority voting triples cost and can converge on a consistent misreading, still shipping unvalidated arithmetic.

questions.rule: Retries fix format and structural errors; they can never fix information that is absent from the source document — classify failures before retrying.
questions.stem: Your validation-retry loop allows 3 retries with error feedback per document. A cost review shows 30% of retry spend goes to receipts failing validation on po_number, which your Pydantic model requires — but receipts do not carry PO numbers, so all three retries fail and the documents route to human review anyway. What change most effectively reduces this waste?
options: Rewrite the retry feedback message to more strongly direct the model to locate the PO number anywhere in the document

Classify validation failures before retrying: make po_number nullable so absent-from-document cases return null with zero retries and route by confidence, while format and structural errors still retry with error feedback
Raise max_retries from 3 to 5 to give the model more attempts on difficult documents
Remove po_number from the schema and validation model entirely so it can never trigger a retry
questions.explanation: Retries are only effective against format and structural errors; no number of retries can produce information the document does not contain. Classifying failure types — making the field nullable so genuinely absent information short-circuits to null with zero retries — is the guide's stated judgment call and eliminates the wasted spend at its root while keeping retries where they work. Strengthening the feedback message intensifies an attempt that is structurally guaranteed to fail: the PO number is not there to find. Raising max_retries to 5 makes the waste 66% worse per affected document. Removing the field entirely solves a different problem by destroying real data — invoices and contracts in the same pipeline do carry PO numbers, and dropping the field loses them everywhere to silence one document type's failure.

questions.rule: Batch failures → resubmit only the failed custom_ids with modifications; batch requests cannot run multi-turn retry conversations in-flight.
questions.stem: Your nightly Message Batches API run processes 6,000 documents. This morning, 45 requests failed with context-length errors on unusually long contracts, and 120 succeeded but failed your semantic validation. A teammate proposes rerunning the entire 6,000-document batch with the validation-retry conversation embedded in each request so failures self-correct in-flight. What is the correct approach?
options: Embed the validation-retry loop inside each batch request so the model corrects failed extractions before the batch completes
Rerun the full 6,000-document batch with a higher max_tokens setting so the long contracts no longer exceed limits

Resubmit only the failed items by custom_id: chunk the 45 oversized contracts, and send the 120 validation failures as new requests that include the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific errors
Migrate the entire nightly pipeline to the synchronous API so retries and long documents can be handled in real time
questions.explanation: custom_id exists precisely to correlate results and enable selective resubmission with modifications — chunking is the standard fix for context-limit failures, and retry feedback goes into new requests because the original conversation cannot continue inside a batch. Embedding the retry loop in batch requests is impossible: the Batch API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request, so no mid-request retry round-trip can execute. Raising max_tokens misdiagnoses the failure — context-length errors come from oversized input, which max_tokens does not address — and rerunning everything wastefully reprocesses 5,835 already-successful documents. Migrating to the synchronous API abandons the 50% batch savings on the entire latency-tolerant nightly volume to fix a problem affecting under 3% of items.

questions.rule: Aggregate accuracy masks segment-level failure — analyze accuracy by document type AND by field before reducing human review.
questions.stem: Your extraction dashboard shows 96.5% overall field accuracy across 40,000 documents per month, comfortably above the 95% target. Leadership wants to cut the human review queue by 80% to reduce costs. What is the most effective first step before reducing review coverage?
options: Raise the confidence threshold for auto-approval so that only the very highest-confidence extractions bypass human review
Proceed with the reduction — 96.5% exceeds the target, and downstream systems will surface any residual errors for correction
Deploy a separately trained error-detection classifier over historical extractions to predict which documents need review
Analyze accuracy segmented by document type and by field to verify performance is consistent across every segment before automating any of them
questions.explanation: A 96.5% aggregate can hide a document type or field running far below target — the guide's core warning before automating high-confidence extractions. Segmenting accuracy by document type and by field is the low-effort, high-leverage verification that must precede any reduction in review. Raising the auto-approval confidence threshold trusts a signal that may be miscalibrated precisely in the failing segments (confidently wrong) and never reveals where errors concentrate. Proceeding on the aggregate shifts detection to downstream systems, where plausible-but-wrong values fail silently for weeks. A separately trained error-detection classifier is over-engineered infrastructure built before running the simple segmentation analysis that actually answers the question.

questions.rule: Lost-in-the-middle: models reliably process the beginning and end of long inputs and drop middles — fix with chunking, summary-first placement, and section headers, not more context capacity.
questions.stem: Your system extracts obligation clauses from 70-page service agreements submitted as a single input. In roughly 20% of documents, clauses from pages 30–50 come back null even though reviewers confirm they are present, while clauses near the beginning and end extract reliably. The documents fit within the context window. What change most effectively addresses this?
options: Chunk each agreement into sections for separate extraction passes, and where aggregated input is required, place a key-information summary at the beginning and organize content with explicit section headers
Switch to a model with a larger context window so the full agreement fits with substantial headroom
Add a system-prompt instruction to "read the entire document carefully, including all middle sections, before extracting any clauses"
Increase max_tokens on the request so the model has enough output budget to extract every clause it finds
questions.explanation: Reliable extraction at the start and end with nulls from the middle is the lost-in-the-middle effect — a position-based attention limitation, not a capacity problem. Chunking into per-section extraction passes, plus summary-first placement and explicit section headers where aggregated input is required, restructures the input around that limitation exactly as the guide prescribes. A larger context window misses the point: the documents already fit, and window capacity does not fix attention position effects. The read-carefully instruction is prompt-only guidance against a probabilistic attention behavior, with no structural change. Raising max_tokens governs output length — nothing indicates truncated output; the model is failing to attend to middle input, not running out of room to respond.

questions.rule: Never route on raw self-reported confidence — calibrate per-field review thresholds against a labeled validation set.
questions.stem: Your extraction tool outputs field-level confidence scores, and you route anything below 0.8 to human review. An audit finds the auto-approved lane carries a 7% error rate on invoice_date fields scored above 0.9, while the human queue is flooded with vendor_name extractions scored around 0.7 that reviewers confirm correct 98% of the time. What change most effectively fixes this routing?
options: Add a system-prompt instruction telling the model to assign confidence scores more conservatively, especially for date fields
Lower the global review threshold from 0.8 to 0.6 to relieve the flooded human queue
Build a labeled validation set, measure actual accuracy at each confidence level per field, and calibrate separate per-field review thresholds from that data
Train a dedicated confidence-estimation model on historical extraction outcomes to replace the extractor's self-reported scores
questions.explanation: The miscalibration runs in opposite directions per field — overconfident on invoice_date, underconfident on vendor_name — so no single raw cutoff can work. The guide's pattern is to measure actual accuracy at each confidence level against a labeled validation set and calibrate separate per-field thresholds, so routing reflects measured accuracy rather than the model's self-assessment. Prompting the model to score more conservatively is prompt-only tuning of a self-reported signal the guide identifies as unreliable — the same failure class as trusting self-reported confidence for escalation. Lowering the global threshold to 0.6 is a symptom patch that shrinks the queue while auto-approving even more confidently wrong invoice_date extractions. A dedicated trained confidence model is over-engineered ML infrastructure when threshold calibration on labeled data has not been tried.

questions.rule: Never stop auditing the automated lane — stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions measures ongoing error rate and catches novel error patterns.
questions.stem: Six months after you automated high-confidence extractions, a major vendor changed its invoice layout. For six weeks, unit prices were extracted into the quantity field — every affected extraction passed validation with high confidence — until an accounting reconciliation finally surfaced the damage. What ongoing process would have caught this weeks earlier?
options: Running each document through two independent extraction calls and auto-approving only when the two results agree
Stratified random sampling of high-confidence auto-approved extractions, with human review of the sample to measure ongoing error rates and detect novel error patterns
Raising the confidence threshold for auto-approval so fewer extractions bypass human review
Restoring full human review of all extractions until the pipeline demonstrates twelve consecutive error-free months
questions.explanation: The layout change produced extractions that were high-confidence, validation-passing, and systematically wrong — a novel error pattern in the automated lane. Stratified random sampling of high-confidence auto-approved output is the guide's mechanism for exactly this: it continuously measures the automated lane's error rate and surfaces new failure modes within the sampling cadence rather than after downstream damage. Dual independent extraction fails because both calls face the same new layout and make the same misreading — agreement does not imply correctness — while doubling cost. A higher confidence threshold does not help when the model is confidently wrong; the bad extractions still clear any cutoff. Restoring full human review for twelve months is wildly disproportionate, abandoning the automation's entire value to compensate for a missing sampling process.
